text: Robotic iliopubic tract repair of indirect inguinal hernia. There has always been a need to perform inguinal hernia repair without mesh. These repairs are typically performed in open anterior fashion. The posterior approach, while not common, is also described. The first description was by Cheadle in This approach was refined and best described in detail as an established technique by Dr. Lloyd Nihus in The Nihus repair constituted suture approximation of the transversalis arch to the iliopubic tract in order to close inguinal defects. This approach was first described laparoscopically by Gazi-Yerli in However, it was noted that the suturing required was very technically challenging using traditional laparoscopic instruments.

The surgical team performs a right-sided robotic inguinal hernia repair with the patient in a supine position. Three 8mm robotic ports are placed after access is obtained with a varus needle. The round ligament and medial umbilical ligaments are identified, followed by visualization of the internal ring with an indirect defect and the right inferior epigastric artery.

The surgeon performs dissection and repair using the robotic TAPP technique, creating a peritoneal flap to access the preperitoneal space. The iliopubic tract lateral to the inferior epigastric artery is visible, followed by lateral dissection revealing the internal ring and indirect defect. The medial space is inspected for direct or femoral components, with defects fully dissected and key structures identified before repair. In female patients, the round ligament is routinely sacrificed.

The surgeon identifies the branches of the genitofemoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve during the repair of an indirect defect. The genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve is visible passing just inferior to the iliopubic tract, while the femoral branch is seen diving inferior to the iliopubic tract, serving as landmarks for the repair.

The surgeon completes the repair by approximating the transversalis arch to the iliopubic tract between the internal ring and the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve. Suturing is performed in an inferior to superior fashion using a running barbed suture, ensuring only the transversus abdominis is incorporated to avoid nerve entrapment. The completed repair of both direct and indirect hernia defects is shown, followed by closure of the peritoneum with a running barbed absorbable suture.
This concludes our video description of the robotic iliopubic tract repair.
